title: practical english usage - Part 119
content:
or about playing musical instruments.

We   often use the + singular when we  talk about musical instruments in general, 10 musical      instruments I worked very hard in the summer  that year.

to use the.

When  we  are talking about particular springs, summers, etc, we  are more likely Rome is lovely in (the) spring. I like (the) winter best.

the summer,  etc. There is little difference.

To  talk about the seasons in general, we  can say spring or the spring, summer  or I was away in April.       We're moving next September.

Where were you last Saturday? See you on Thursday.

We   drop the when we  mean 'the day/month before or after this one’ .

9 days,    months      and   seasons How did man first discover fire?

regard this usage as sexist and prefer to avoid it (►   328.6).

Man is also commonly used to mean 'the human race’ , though many people Men and women have similar abilities and needs.

A woman without a man is like a fish without a bicycle. (old feminist joke) But we  more often use a woman and a man, or men and women.

Man and woman were created equal.

general sense without articles.

Unlike other singular countable nouns, man and woman can be used in a 8  man     and   woman The number of unemployed is rising steadily.

I was surprised at the amount of money collected. (     .. . of the money) The is dropped after the ɑmount/number of.

7 amount         and   number grammar •  142   special rules and exceptions He sits watching TV by the hour.  Can I pay by the month?       “ > Do you sell eggs by the kilo or by the dozen?

Note the use of the in measuring expressions beginning with by.

17 measurements:          by   the    hour;     twice      a  week Can't you look me in the eye?

She hit him  in the stomach. He was shot in the leg.

(►   143.5).

blows, pains and other things that often happen to parts of people’ s bodies But the is common after prepositions, especially when we  are talking about not Katy broke her arm climbing, (     Katy broke the armdimbing) the arm.) not He stood in the doorway, his coat over his arm. (     ... the eeat over use possessives, not the.

When talking about someone’ s possessions, or parts of their body, we  usually 16 parts    of  the   body,    etc (BrE I've got toothache/earache, etc) I've got a toothache / an earache / a backache / a stomach ache.

American usage is different in some cases.

She's never had (the) measles.

I think I've got (the) flu. (AmE always the flu) The can be used informally with a few common illnesses.

I've got a horrible cold. Have you got a headache?

A/an is used in a few cases such as a cold, a headache.

Have you had appendicitis? I've got toothache again.

In standard British English (for more details, ►     119.7).

The  names of illnesses and pains are usually uncountable, with no article.

What nonsense! (     What a nonsense!) What luck!

Note that a/an cannot be used in exclamations with uncountable nouns.

What a lovely dress! (     What-lovely- dress!) We use ɑ/ɑn  with singular countable nouns in exclamations after What.

14 exclamations:         What      a  .. .!

Good morning, children, (     Good-morning,-the- children.) the group.

We don’ t address groups of people directly by using the with the name of 13 addressing        people:    Good     morning,         children.

He was elected President in 1879.    I want to see the president.

They appointed him  Head Librarian. Where's the librarian?

somebody has or gains a unique position (the only one in the organisation).

And the is not usually used in the complement of a sentence, when we  say that The Queen had dinner with the President.

Queen Elizabeth had dinner with President Obama.

The is not used in titles like Queen Elizabeth, President Obama. Compare: 12 jobs    and   positions:      He  was     elected        President.
